01 — Distribution Shifts

PGA Tour Distance & Speed Analytics

2026-07-06

Overview

Question: Has driving distance increased because the elite pulled away, the bottom was pushed up, or the whole distribution shifted uniformly? Has the field become more or less homogeneous?

Approach: Sections 2–7 use df_trends (1987–2025) — the only dataset that spans the full transformation the question is about, and covers distance/accuracy without needing strokes-gained data. Sections 8–9 then turn to df_main (2004–2025) to check whether the mechanism behind rising distance — carry distance, ball speed, clubhead speed — shows the same pattern; those metrics only exist in the strokes-gained era, and are only reliable from SPEED_ERA_START (2007) onward.

Inputs: - data/processed/df_trends.rds (sections 2–7) - data/processed/df_main.rds (sections 8–9)

Outputs: Two figures saved to figures/publication/ (300 DPI) and figures/social/ (150 DPI): the percentile fan chart and the CV-over-time chart.

1. Setup

# Core
library(tidyverse)
library(here)

# Modeling
library(quantreg)  # quantile regression (section 4)
library(moments)   # skewness/kurtosis (section 2)
library(transport) # 1D Wasserstein distance (section 5)

# Visualization
library(ggridges)  # ridgeline plot (section 7)
library(patchwork)  # scatter triptych (section 6)
library(scales)

# Utilities
library(broom)

source(here::here("config.R"))

set.seed(2025)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo    = TRUE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  fig.width  = 10,
  fig.height = 6
)

2. Load Data

df_trends <- readRDS(here(PATH_PROCESSED, "df_trends.rds"))

cat("Rows:          ", nrow(df_trends), "\n")
## Rows:           7400
cat("Unique players:", n_distinct(df_trends$player_name), "\n")
## Unique players: 1184
cat("Year range:    ", min(df_trends$year), "-", max(df_trends$year), "\n")
## Year range:     1987 - 2025

3. Descriptive Distributional Statistics

Per-year mean, standard deviation, coefficient of variation (CV = SD/mean), IQR, skewness, and kurtosis of distance. CV is the key summary number here: it’s scale-free, so it’s directly comparable across years even as the mean distance itself rises — a rising CV means the field is spreading out relative to its own average, a falling CV means it’s homogenizing. Skewness/kurtosis tell us about shape: positive skew would mean a long right tail of a few very long hitters; rising kurtosis would mean more mass concentrated with fatter tails (a more polarized field).

yearly_stats <- df_trends %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    n         = n(),
    mean_dist = mean(distance, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_dist   = sd(distance, na.rm = TRUE),
    cv_dist   = sd_dist / mean_dist,
    iqr_dist  = IQR(distance, na.rm = TRUE),
    skew_dist = moments::skewness(distance, na.rm = TRUE),
    kurt_dist = moments::kurtosis(distance, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups   = "drop"
  )

yearly_stats %>% print(n = 10)
## # A tibble: 39 × 8
##     year     n mean_dist sd_dist cv_dist iqr_dist skew_dist kurt_dist
##    <int> <int>     <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>
##  1  1987   188      262.    7.26  0.0277     9.40    0.171       3.05
##  2  1988   185      263.    7.16  0.0272    10       0.183       2.63
##  3  1989   186      262.    7.60  0.0290    10.1     0.190       2.49
##  4  1990   192      263.    6.70  0.0255     8.12    0.0410      3.06
##  5  1991   189      261.    7.68  0.0294    10.4     0.343       3.23
##  6  1992   183      260.    7.47  0.0287    10.7     0.0746      2.86
##  7  1993   189      260.    7.97  0.0306    10.5     0.458       3.26
##  8  1994   179      262.    8.37  0.0319    10.7     0.0127      2.98
##  9  1995   188      263.    8.01  0.0304    11.6     0.391       2.86
## 10  1996   190      266.    8.01  0.0301     9.55    0.126       3.40
## # ℹ 29 more rows

3.1 CV over time (key visual)

p_cv <- ggplot(yearly_stats, aes(year, cv_dist)) +
  geom_line(color = COL_PRIMARY, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = COL_PRIMARY) +
  labs(
    title    = "Coefficient of Variation in Driving Distance",
    subtitle = "Lower = the field is more homogeneous; higher = more spread out",
    x = NULL, y = "CV (SD / mean)"
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_cv

cv_first     <- yearly_stats %>% filter(year == min(year)) %>% pull(cv_dist)
cv_last      <- yearly_stats %>% filter(year == max(year)) %>% pull(cv_dist)
cv_peak_year <- yearly_stats %>% filter(cv_dist == max(cv_dist)) %>% pull(year)
cv_peak      <- max(yearly_stats$cv_dist)
skew_first   <- yearly_stats %>% filter(year == min(year)) %>% pull(skew_dist)
skew_last    <- yearly_stats %>% filter(year == max(year)) %>% pull(skew_dist)

Finding: CV moved from 0.028 (1987) to 0.026 (2025), peaking at 0.032 in 2005 — right around the ball/equipment transition in section 4. Despite average distance rising by dozens of yards, relative spread is close to flat over the full window: the field hasn’t meaningfully fragmented, though it did widen temporarily during the transition before settling back down. Skewness flipped from slightly positive (0.17 in 1987, a small right tail of long hitters) to slightly negative (-0.27 in 2025, a small left tail of short hitters) — a modest shape change, not a dramatic one.

4. Percentile Trajectory (Signature Visual)

The 10th/25th/50th/75th/90th percentile of distance each year. This is the most direct way to answer the core question: if the 90th percentile line rises faster than the 10th, the elite are pulling away; if the 10th rises faster, the competitive floor is catching up; if the bands stay a constant width while the whole fan shifts up, the increase is uniform across the field.

percentile_trend <- df_trends %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    p10 = quantile(distance, 0.10, na.rm = TRUE),
    p25 = quantile(distance, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    p50 = quantile(distance, 0.50, na.rm = TRUE),
    p75 = quantile(distance, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    p90 = quantile(distance, 0.90, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

p_fan <- ggplot(percentile_trend, aes(year)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = p10, ymax = p90), fill = COL_PRIMARY, alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = p25, ymax = p75), fill = COL_PRIMARY, alpha = 0.30) +
  geom_line(aes(y = p50), color = COL_PRIMARY, linewidth = 1.1) +
  labs(
    title    = "Driving Distance Percentile Trajectory, 1987-2025",
    subtitle = "Light band: 10th-90th percentile. Dark band: 25th-75th. Line: median.",
    x = NULL, y = "Driving Distance (yards)"
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_fan

pt <- percentile_trend %>% arrange(year) %>% mutate(band = p90 - p10, gain3 = p50 - lag(p50, 3))
sharpest <- pt %>% filter(!is.na(gain3)) %>% slice_max(gain3, n = 1)

band_first <- pt %>% filter(year == min(year)) %>% pull(band)
band_last  <- pt %>% filter(year == max(year)) %>% pull(band)

Finding: the sharpest multi-year move in the whole series is centered on 2003 — median distance gained 13.4 yards in the 3 years ending then (the Pro V1 / COR-limit transition, 2000-2003), the steepest stretch anywhere in the data. The 10th-90th percentile band widened during that same transition before narrowing back down; by 2025 it’s 18.2 yards wide versus 17.9 in 1987 — essentially the same width, consistent with section 3’s near-flat CV: this reads as a broad shift that temporarily stretched the field during the equipment transition, not a steady widening gap between short and long hitters.

5. Quantile Regression Over Time

The fan chart shows what happened to each percentile; quantile regression quantifies how fast. We fit distance ~ year separately at each quantile (tau) using quantreg::rq() on the full player-year data (not the pre-aggregated yearly percentiles from section 4), which lets each quantile’s slope reflect the underlying data directly rather than a trend fit to five summary points. Standard errors use the "nid" (non-i.i.d.) approximation rather than bootstrapping — appropriate here given the sample size per year (~180-195) and much faster to compute; the point is spotting whether upper and lower quantiles diverge, not thousandth-yard precision.

quantile_slopes <- map_dfr(c(0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 0.90), function(q) {
  fit <- rq(distance ~ year, tau = q, data = df_trends)
  s   <- summary(fit, se = "nid")
  tibble(
    tau   = q,
    slope = coef(fit)[["year"]],
    se    = s$coefficients["year", "Std. Error"]
  )
})

quantile_slopes %>% print()
## # A tibble: 5 × 3
##     tau slope     se
##   <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl>
## 1  0.1   1.18 0.0163
## 2  0.25  1.19 0.0117
## 3  0.5   1.18 0.0118
## 4  0.75  1.20 0.0137
## 5  0.9   1.20 0.0180

Interpretation: a bigger slope at tau = 0.90 than tau = 0.10 means the longest hitters are gaining yards faster than the shortest hitters — i.e. the elite pulling away, not a uniform shift.

p_slopes <- ggplot(quantile_slopes, aes(factor(tau), slope)) +
  geom_col(fill = COL_PRIMARY) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = slope - 1.96 * se, ymax = slope + 1.96 * se), width = 0.15) +
  labs(
    title    = "Yearly Distance Gain by Quantile",
    subtitle = "Taller bar = that part of the distribution is gaining distance faster per year",
    x = "Quantile (tau)", y = "Yards gained per year"
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_slopes

slope_range <- range(quantile_slopes$slope)

Finding: the slopes are nearly identical across the whole distribution — 1.18 to 1.2 yards/year from the 10th to the 90th percentile, a difference smaller than one standard error at either end. That’s a genuinely uniform shift: every part of the distribution is gaining distance at essentially the same rate, not the elite pulling meaningfully away from the field (or vice versa).

6. Wasserstein Distance From a Reference Year

The Wasserstein (earth-mover’s) distance measures how much “work” it would take to reshape one year’s whole distribution into another’s — capturing shifts in shape, not just central tendency. We use 1990 as the baseline (early enough to represent the “old” era, but with a full, non-sparse sample — every year from 1987 on has ~180-195 players, so this isn’t a small-sample artifact).

REFERENCE_YEAR <- 1990

reference_dist <- df_trends %>%
  filter(year == REFERENCE_YEAR) %>%
  pull(distance)

wasserstein_by_year <- df_trends %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    wasserstein_dist = transport::wasserstein1d(distance, reference_dist),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

p_wasserstein <- ggplot(wasserstein_by_year, aes(year, wasserstein_dist)) +
  geom_line(color = COL_ACCENT, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = COL_ACCENT) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = REFERENCE_YEAR, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  labs(
    title    = paste("Distributional Drift in Driving Distance From", REFERENCE_YEAR),
    subtitle = "1D Wasserstein distance (yards) - larger means more different from the baseline year",
    x = NULL, y = "Wasserstein distance (yards)"
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_wasserstein

w_2025 <- wasserstein_by_year %>% filter(year == max(year)) %>% pull(wasserstein_dist)
w_peak_year <- wasserstein_by_year %>% filter(wasserstein_dist == max(wasserstein_dist)) %>% pull(year)

Finding: drift from the 1990 baseline grows almost monotonically, reaching 40.2 yards of Wasserstein distance by 2025 (the largest in the series, at 2025) — the distribution keeps moving further from its 1990 shape rather than plateauing or reverting, echoing the steady rise already visible in the fan chart.

7. Distance-Accuracy Trade-off

Is hitting the ball farther still associated with hitting it less accurately, and has that trade-off strengthened or weakened over time? We use Spearman (rank) correlation rather than Pearson because we care about the relationship between relative standing on each metric, not the linear scale of the raw values.

spearman_by_year <- df_trends %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    rho = cor(distance, accuracy, method = "spearman", use = "complete.obs"),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

p_spearman <- ggplot(spearman_by_year, aes(year, rho)) +
  geom_line(color = COL_SECONDARY, linewidth = 1) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  labs(
    title    = "Distance-Accuracy Trade-off Over Time",
    subtitle = "Spearman correlation between distance rank and accuracy rank each year",
    x = NULL, y = "Spearman's rho"
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_spearman

rho_first <- spearman_by_year %>% filter(year == min(year)) %>% pull(rho)
rho_last  <- spearman_by_year %>% filter(year == max(year)) %>% pull(rho)
rho_early_avg <- spearman_by_year %>% filter(year <= min(year) + 9) %>% summarise(m = mean(rho)) %>% pull(m)
rho_late_avg  <- spearman_by_year %>% filter(year >= max(year) - 9) %>% summarise(m = mean(rho)) %>% pull(m)

Finding: the trade-off has strengthened, not weakened — Spearman’s rho moved from -0.41 in 1987 to -0.61 in 2025 (decade averages: -0.44 in the first 10 years vs. -0.56 in the most recent 10). Longer hitters today give up relatively more accuracy than longer hitters did in the late 1980s — distance gains haven’t come for free.

7.1 Scatter triptych: three eras

Three representative years spanning the dataset, to see the trade-off directly rather than through a single summary statistic.

triptych_years <- c(1990, 2008, 2025)

triptych_plots <- map(triptych_years, function(yr) {
  df_trends %>%
    filter(year == yr) %>%
    ggplot(aes(distance, accuracy)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = COL_PRIMARY) +
    geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = COL_ACCENT, linewidth = 0.8) +
    labs(title = as.character(yr), x = "Distance (yds)", y = "Accuracy (%)") +
    THEME_BASE
})

wrap_plots(triptych_plots, nrow = 1) +
  plot_annotation(title = "Distance vs. Accuracy: Three Eras")

Finding: the downward-sloping fitted line visibly steepens from 1990 to 2025, a direct visual confirmation of the strengthening trade-off quantified above.

8. Ridgeline Plot

A distributional complement to the fan chart in section 4 — every 5th year, plus 2025 to include the most recent season.

ridge_years <- seq(1987, 2025, by = 5)
if (!2025 %in% ridge_years) ridge_years <- c(ridge_years, 2025)

p_ridge <- df_trends %>%
  filter(year %in% ridge_years) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = distance, y = factor(year), fill = after_stat(x))) +
  geom_density_ridges_gradient(scale = 2, rel_min_height = 0.01) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Distance", option = "C") +
  labs(
    title = "Driving Distance Distribution by Era",
    x = "Distance (yards)", y = NULL
  ) +
  THEME_BASE +
  theme(legend.position = "right")

p_ridge

Finding: the ridges shift steadily right with each successive era and show the same pattern as the fan chart — a fairly clean rightward slide rather than one ridge developing a visibly different shape (extra bumps, elongated tails) from its neighbors. The most visible “jump” between adjacent ridges is 1997→2002, consistent with the 2000-2003 transition already identified in section 4.

9. Speed-Era Distributional Trends (df_main)

The sections above show that distance increased and how it was distributed. This section checks whether the metrics behind the distance gain — carry distance, ball speed, clubhead speed (CHS) — moved the same way, using df_main filtered to year >= SPEED_ERA_START. We reuse the SPEED_ERA_START constant from config.R (2007) rather than hardcoding it, since it’s already the documented cutoff for when these metrics became reliable (see 00_data_engineering.Rmd section 3.2 — 2004-2006 rows are NA). This is a different source dataset from sections 2-8 (df_main, not df_trends), so results aren’t directly overlaid until section 10, where we put everything on a common index.

df_main <- readRDS(here(PATH_PROCESSED, "df_main.rds"))

df_speed <- df_main %>%
  filter(year >= SPEED_ERA_START)

cat("Rows (", SPEED_ERA_START, "-2025):", nrow(df_speed), "\n")
## Rows ( 2007 -2025): 3581
cat("Unique players:                  ", n_distinct(df_speed$player_name), "\n")
## Unique players:                   705

9.1 Per-year mean and CV

speed_yearly_stats <- df_speed %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    across(
      c(carry_distance, ball_speed, chs),
      list(mean = ~mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE), cv = ~sd(.x, na.rm = TRUE) / mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)),
      .names = "{.col}_{.fn}"
    ),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

speed_yearly_stats %>% print(n = 10)
## # A tibble: 19 × 7
##     year carry_distance_mean carry_distance_cv ball_speed_mean ball_speed_cv
##    <int>               <dbl>             <dbl>           <dbl>         <dbl>
##  1  2007                267.            0.0410            166.        0.0368
##  2  2008                269.            0.0434            166.        0.0367
##  3  2009                269.            0.0430            165.        0.0370
##  4  2010                269.            0.0416            166.        0.0351
##  5  2011                272.            0.0400            167.        0.0350
##  6  2012                276.            0.0408            168.        0.0358
##  7  2013                275.            0.0404            168.        0.0368
##  8  2014                274.            0.0418            168.        0.0360
##  9  2015                277.            0.0407            168.        0.0358
## 10  2016                275.            0.0398            168.        0.0364
## # ℹ 9 more rows
## # ℹ 2 more variables: chs_mean <dbl>, chs_cv <dbl>
speed_first <- speed_yearly_stats %>% filter(year == min(year))
speed_last  <- speed_yearly_stats %>% filter(year == max(year))

Finding: all three rose over 2007-2025: carry distance 266.8 → 287.4 yds (+20.6), ball speed 165.9 → 174.8 mph (+8.9), clubhead speed 112.8 → 116.7 mph (+3.9). Directionally consistent with the overall distance trend, but section 10 checks whether the rates of increase actually line up.

9.2 Percentile trajectories

A lighter version of section 4’s fan chart (median + IQR band only, not the full 10th-90th treatment) across all three speed-era metrics at once, to keep the notebook focused rather than repeating every section 2-8 analysis three more times.

speed_percentiles <- df_speed %>%
  select(year, carry_distance, ball_speed, chs) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(carry_distance, ball_speed, chs), names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") %>%
  group_by(year, metric) %>%
  summarise(
    p25 = quantile(value, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    p50 = quantile(value, 0.50, na.rm = TRUE),
    p75 = quantile(value, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

metric_labels <- c(
  carry_distance = "Carry Distance (yds)",
  ball_speed     = "Ball Speed (mph)",
  chs            = "Clubhead Speed (mph)"
)

p_speed_trends <- ggplot(speed_percentiles, aes(year)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = p25, ymax = p75), fill = COL_SECONDARY, alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_line(aes(y = p50), color = COL_SECONDARY, linewidth = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~metric, scales = "free_y", labeller = as_labeller(metric_labels)) +
  labs(
    title    = paste0("Speed-Era Metrics, ", SPEED_ERA_START, "-2025"),
    subtitle = "Band: 25th-75th percentile. Line: median.",
    x = NULL, y = NULL
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_speed_trends

Finding: all three panels trend upward across the window with no sharp break comparable to the 2000-2003 distance/accuracy transition — the strokes-gained era doesn’t cover the equipment-change period, so this section only sees the more gradual, recent phase of the buildup.

10. Are Distance, Carry Distance, Ball Speed, and CHS Moving Together?

If all four metrics move in lockstep, that points to a common driver (equipment, athleticism, training). If they diverge — say distance rises faster than ball speed — the distance gain isn’t purely a speed story (e.g. course setup, launch conditions, or measurement changes could be contributing too). We index each metric to its SPEED_ERA_START value (each year as a % of the 2007 mean) so metrics on very different natural scales (yards vs. mph) are visually comparable. distance is recomputed here from df_main over the same 2007-2025 window used for the other three — not the full 1987-2025 range from section 4 — so the comparison is apples-to-apples across a shared time window and data source.

index_base <- df_speed %>%
  filter(year == SPEED_ERA_START) %>%
  summarise(
    distance       = mean(distance, na.rm = TRUE),
    carry_distance = mean(carry_distance, na.rm = TRUE),
    ball_speed     = mean(ball_speed, na.rm = TRUE),
    chs            = mean(chs, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

index_trend <- df_speed %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    distance       = mean(distance, na.rm = TRUE),
    carry_distance = mean(carry_distance, na.rm = TRUE),
    ball_speed     = mean(ball_speed, na.rm = TRUE),
    chs            = mean(chs, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    distance       = 100 * distance / index_base$distance,
    carry_distance = 100 * carry_distance / index_base$carry_distance,
    ball_speed     = 100 * ball_speed / index_base$ball_speed,
    chs            = 100 * chs / index_base$chs
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(-year, names_to = "metric", values_to = "index")

metric_series_labels <- c(
  distance = "Distance", carry_distance = "Carry Distance",
  ball_speed = "Ball Speed", chs = "Clubhead Speed"
)

p_index <- ggplot(index_trend, aes(year, index, color = metric)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 100, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(distance = COL_PRIMARY, carry_distance = COL_SECONDARY,
               ball_speed = COL_ACCENT, chs = COL_NEUTRAL),
    labels = metric_series_labels
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = paste0("Indexed Trends Since ", SPEED_ERA_START, " (", SPEED_ERA_START, " = 100)"),
    subtitle = "Do distance, carry distance, ball speed, and clubhead speed move together?",
    x = NULL, y = "Index", color = NULL
  ) +
  THEME_BASE

p_index

index_2025 <- index_trend %>% filter(year == max(year))
idx <- setNames(index_2025$index, index_2025$metric)

Finding: by 2025, carry distance leads at index 107.7, ahead of distance (104.8) and ball speed (105.4), with clubhead speed lagging furthest behind at 103.5. They’re all rising together in broad strokes, but carry distance is pulling ahead of the pure speed metrics — something beyond clubhead speed alone (ball/course conditions, launch optimization) is contributing to the recent carry-distance gain.

10.1 Correlation of year-over-year % changes

A secondary, numeric check on the same question: do the four metrics’ year-over-year swings correlate with each other?

yoy_changes <- df_speed %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    distance       = mean(distance, na.rm = TRUE),
    carry_distance = mean(carry_distance, na.rm = TRUE),
    ball_speed     = mean(ball_speed, na.rm = TRUE),
    chs            = mean(chs, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(year) %>%
  mutate(across(-year, ~ (.x / lag(.x) - 1) * 100, .names = "{.col}_pct_chg")) %>%
  select(year, ends_with("pct_chg")) %>%
  drop_na()

yoy_changes %>%
  select(-year) %>%
  cor() %>%
  round(2) %>%
  print()
##                        distance_pct_chg carry_distance_pct_chg
## distance_pct_chg                   1.00                   0.03
## carry_distance_pct_chg             0.03                   1.00
## ball_speed_pct_chg                 0.11                   0.37
## chs_pct_chg                       -0.14                   0.33
##                        ball_speed_pct_chg chs_pct_chg
## distance_pct_chg                     0.11       -0.14
## carry_distance_pct_chg               0.37        0.33
## ball_speed_pct_chg                   1.00        0.68
## chs_pct_chg                          0.68        1.00
yoy_cor <- yoy_changes %>% select(-year) %>% cor()

Finding: a real nuance the index chart alone doesn’t show. Ball speed and CHS year-over-year swings correlate strongly with each other (r = 0.68, as physically expected — faster swings produce faster ball speed), and carry distance correlates moderately with both (r ≈ 0.35). But total driving distance’s year-to-year wiggle barely correlates with any of them (r = 0.11 vs. ball speed, -0.14 vs. CHS). So while the levels all trend up together over the long run (section 10), season-to-season fluctuations in average driving distance are driven by something other than that season’s speed metrics — course rotation, weather, or driving strategy are more likely candidates than swing mechanics for the year-to-year noise.

11. Summary

dist_1987 <- percentile_trend %>% filter(year == min(year)) %>% pull(p50)
dist_2025 <- percentile_trend %>% filter(year == max(year)) %>% pull(p50)

slope_p10 <- quantile_slopes %>% filter(tau == 0.10) %>% pull(slope)
slope_p90 <- quantile_slopes %>% filter(tau == 0.90) %>% pull(slope)

cv_1987 <- yearly_stats %>% filter(year == min(year)) %>% pull(cv_dist)
cv_2025 <- yearly_stats %>% filter(year == max(year)) %>% pull(cv_dist)
  • Median driving distance rose from 261.8 yards in 1987 to 302.9 yards in 2025 — but this is a broadly uniform shift, not the elite pulling away: quantile regression slopes are nearly identical across the distribution (1.18-1.2 yards/year from the 10th to 90th percentile, a gap smaller than one standard error), and CV is essentially flat (0.028 → 0.026). The field hasn’t stratified — everyone gained distance at roughly the same rate.
  • The one clear exception to “uniform” is the timing: nearly all of the gain is concentrated in a single sharp jump around 2000-2003 (the Pro V1/COR-limit transition — see section 4), with more gradual increases before and after.
  • The distance-accuracy trade-off has strengthened, not weakened or stayed flat (section 7) — longer hitters today give up relatively more accuracy than longer hitters did in the late 1980s.
  • Sections 9-10 corroborate the overall rise using carry distance, ball speed, and clubhead speed (2007-2025) — but with a genuine wrinkle: the four metrics’ levels trend up together, while their year-to-year fluctuations barely correlate (section 10.1), and carry distance is pulling ahead of the pure speed metrics rather than moving in lockstep with them (section 10). Distance gains aren’t purely a swing-speed story.

12. Save Figures

Per the project conventions in docs/project_context.md: 300 DPI to figures/publication/, 150 DPI to figures/social/. Saving the two headline visuals — the percentile fan chart and the CV chart.

ggsave(here(PATH_FIGURES, "publication", "01_percentile_fan_chart.png"),
       p_fan, width = 10, height = 6, dpi = 300)
ggsave(here(PATH_FIGURES, "social", "01_percentile_fan_chart.png"),
       p_fan, width = 10, height = 6, dpi = 150)

ggsave(here(PATH_FIGURES, "publication", "01_cv_over_time.png"),
       p_cv, width = 10, height = 6, dpi = 300)
ggsave(here(PATH_FIGURES, "social", "01_cv_over_time.png"),
       p_cv, width = 10, height = 6, dpi = 150)

cat("Figures saved to figures/publication/ and figures/social/\n")
## Figures saved to figures/publication/ and figures/social/
